title: Leviathan
author: Hobbes, Thomas
summary: Thomas Hobbes presents human beings as sense-driven, imaginative, and passionate creatures whose fear of violent death leads them to contract together. By mutual covenant they create an artificial person, the Leviathan or commonwealth, whose sovereign functions as artificial soul. Magistrates, laws, rewards, and punishments correspond to a body’s members and nerves, securing peace. Hobbes develops the psychology of perception, speech, reason, and the laws of nature, and argues for absolute authority to prevent civil war. He examines government types, succession, ecclesiastical power, and the relation of religion to state order, warning against sedition and doctrinal confusion to preserve social stability.

characters.name: Thomas Hobbes
characters.personality: Analytic, polemical, systematic philosopher and author
characters.lifespan: Author and narrator of the treatise; survives as the text's voice

characters.name: Mr. Francis Godolphin
characters.lifespan: Recipient of the dedication; mentioned in the Preface

characters.name: Mr. Sidney Godolphin
characters.lifespan: Referred to in the dedication as deceased and honoured

characters.name: God (the Artificer)
characters.personality: Creator and governor of nature, invoked as ultimate maker
characters.lifespan: Eternal; invoked as the natural Artificer of the world

characters.name: Leviathan (the Commonwealth)
characters.personality: Powerful, artificial personification of the state
characters.lifespan: Constructed by covenant; endures while the commonwealth stands

characters.name: The Sovereign (artificial soul)
characters.personality: Authoritative, commanding, source of unity and motion in the state
characters.lifespan: Maintains life of the commonwealth while obeyed

characters.name: Magistrates
characters.personality: Executive, judicial officers; instruments of sovereign will
characters.lifespan: Serve under the sovereign within the commonwealth

characters.name: Counsellors
characters.personality: Advisory, memory-like function for the state
characters.lifespan: Function as long as the commonwealth requires counsel

characters.name: Particular Members / Subjects
characters.personality: Individual citizens driven by passions and reason
characters.lifespan: Contribute to and are protected by the commonwealth while it exists

characters.name: Nature
characters.personality: Described as the art by which God made and governs the world
characters.lifespan: Ongoing; used as explanatory principle in the text

characters.name: Our Blessed Saviour
characters.personality: Religious figure discussed in relation to Christian commonwealth
characters.lifespan: Referred to in theological chapters and Christian-political analysis

characters.name: Abraham
characters.personality: Patriarch cited in discussion of kingdom rights
characters.lifespan: Appears as scriptural example in church-state chapters

characters.name: Moses
characters.personality: Lawgiver cited regarding rights and authority
characters.lifespan: Appears as scriptural example in theological discussion

characters.name: High Priests
characters.personality: Religious authorities discussed in relation to political rights
characters.lifespan: Discussed historically and doctrinally in the book

characters.name: Kings of Judah
characters.personality: Monarchs used as examples for royal rights and authority
characters.lifespan: Historic actors invoked in theological-political chapters

unusual_words.definition: Archaic spelling of artificial; made by art or human design
unusual_words.context: Used to describe the constructed 'Artificiall Man' (the commonwealth)

unusual_words.definition: Self-moving machines or mechanisms
unusual_words.context: Compares natural life to 'Automata' and watch-like mechanisms

unusual_words.definition: Biblical sea monster; here the title and metaphor for the state
unusual_words.context: Title and central metaphor: the commonwealth as a great sea-beast/artificial person

unusual_words.definition: A political community founded for common good (hyphenated archaic form)
unusual_words.context: Used as the principal institutional object Hobbes describes

unusual_words.definition: Latin for city or state
unusual_words.context: Given in parentheses to translate commonwealth

unusual_words.definition: Pertaining to the church or clergy
unusual_words.context: Part of the book's extended subtitle

unusual_words.definition: Archaic spelling of civil, relating to citizens or the state
unusual_words.context: Appears in the subtitle describing civil power

unusual_words.definition: Maker or skilled creator; here God as creator
unusual_words.context: God is called the Artificer who made the natural man

unusual_words.definition: Archaic spelling of sovereignty; supreme authority
unusual_words.context: Describes the sovereign as the artificial soul of the commonwealth

unusual_words.definition: Public officials who administer law
unusual_words.context: Described as artificial joints of the commonwealth-body

unusual_words.definition: Administration of justice; judiciary
unusual_words.context: Used to label parts of the state apparatus

unusual_words.definition: Enforcement of laws and judgments
unusual_words.context: Listed among functions of magistrates and officers

unusual_words.definition: Archaic spelling of joints, parts connecting a body
unusual_words.context: Used in the bodily metaphor for magistrates and officers

unusual_words.definition: Archaic spelling of wheels; mechanical parts enabling motion
unusual_words.context: Used in the machine metaphor (wheels of a watch)

unusual_words.definition: Literal bodily nerves; metaphorically means instruments of motion
unusual_words.context: Rewards and punishments are compared to nerves moving the body

unusual_words.definition: Archaic spelling of business; public concern or activity
unusual_words.context: Refers to the commonwealth's purpose: 'its Businesse'

unusual_words.definition: Advisers or counselors to the sovereign
unusual_words.context: Compared to memory in the artificial commonwealth

unusual_words.definition: Faculty of retaining and recalling knowledge
unusual_words.context: Metaphorically assigned to counsellors in the state

unusual_words.definition: Fairness or justice in the application of law
unusual_words.context: Described as an artificial reason and will

unusual_words.definition: Archaic plural of laws
unusual_words.context: Used in headings and text discussing civil law

unusual_words.definition: Agreement, harmony among members of the state
unusual_words.context: Named as health of the commonwealth

unusual_words.definition: Conduct inciting rebellion against authority
unusual_words.context: Described as a disease weakening the body politic

unusual_words.definition: Archaic spelling of sickness or illness
unusual_words.context: Metaphor for state disorder; sedition causes sicknesse

unusual_words.definition: Armed conflict between factions within a state
unusual_words.context: Analogized to death of the body politic

unusual_words.definition: Agreements or treaties; here foundational covenants
unusual_words.context: Pacts and covenants form the body politic

unusual_words.definition: Formal agreements creating mutual obligations
unusual_words.context: Central mechanism by which the commonwealth is built

unusual_words.definition: A formal decree or 'let it be done' (Latin)
unusual_words.context: Compared to God's 'Let Us Make Man' in creation metaphor

unusual_words.definition: Variant of Latin 'Nosce te ipsum' ('know thyself')
unusual_words.context: Quoted in the Introduction: 'Nosce Teipsum, Read Thy Self'

unusual_words.definition: The natural order; God's workmanship
unusual_words.context: Defined as the art by which God made and governs the world

unusual_words.definition: Human skill or craft; imitation of nature
unusual_words.context: Contrasted with natural creation to explain the artificial commonwealth

unusual_words.definition: Archaic spelling of inhere; to be inherent or innate
unusual_words.context: Used describing virtues shining 'inhaerent' in Sidney Godolphin

unusual_words.definition: Clearly and plainly; with perspicuity
unusual_words.context: Hobbes promises to set down his reading 'orderly, and perspicuously'

unusual_words.definition: One by one; individually
unusual_words.context: Chapter I: 'Concerning the Thoughts of man, I will consider them first Singly...'

unusual_words.definition: Archaic spelling of train (sequence or series)
unusual_words.context: Used in the phrase 'in Trayne, or dependance upon one another'

unusual_words.definition: Archaic spelling of appearance
unusual_words.context: Used describing mental representations: 'every one a Representation or Apparence'

unusual_words.definition: That toward which perception or thought is directed
unusual_words.context: Appears discussing the objects that produce mental apparences

unusual_words.definition: An attribute or quality that is not essential
unusual_words.context: Used philosophically: 'some quality, or other Accident of a body without us'

unusual_words.definition: Perception via the senses; origin of all conceptions
unusual_words.context: Chapter I begins by establishing sense as origin of thought

unusual_words.definition: Archaic spelling of taste (sense of taste)
unusual_words.context: Listed among senses: 'as in the Tast and Touch'

unusual_words.definition: Sense of tactile perception
unusual_words.context: Paired with taste as an immediate pressure on organs

unusual_words.definition: Archaic spelling of ears
unusual_words.context: Mentioned among organs receiving external impressions

unusual_words.definition: Sense of smell
unusual_words.context: Listed among senses such as seeing and hearing

unusual_words.definition: Sense of sight
unusual_words.context: Included in list of mediate senses

unusual_words.definition: Anatomical organ; seat of perception in Hobbes' physiology
unusual_words.context: External pressures are transmitted inward to the Brain and Heart

unusual_words.definition: Physiological organ; considered origin of motion in Hobbes' account
unusual_words.context: The heart's endeavour produces the fancy of outward matter

unusual_words.definition: A bodily instrument or sense organ
unusual_words.context: Pressure on the organ proper to each sense

unusual_words.definition: Physical force exerted by external bodies
unusual_words.context: External bodies press organs to produce sense

unusual_words.definition: An effort or striving (archaic spelling preserved)
unusual_words.context: Heart's counter-pressure or 'endeavour' to deliver itself

unusual_words.definition: Appearance or appearance of a thing
unusual_words.context: This 'Seeming, or Fancy' constitutes mental representation

unusual_words.definition: Imagination or mental image
unusual_words.context: Used interchangeably with seeming for impressions of objects

unusual_words.definition: Caused or produced (often used for generation)
unusual_words.context: Concepts are 'begotten upon the organs of Sense'

unusual_words.definition: Plural form of organ; bodily instruments
unusual_words.context: Sense is produced by external bodies acting on organs

unusual_words.definition: Formation of an idea or notion
unusual_words.context: Hobbes: 'there is no conception in a mans mind, which hath not at first... been begotten upon the organs of Sense'

unusual_words.definition: Mental faculty or thinking subject
unusual_words.context: Used as seat of conception and imagination

unusual_words.definition: Constituent elements of a whole
unusual_words.context: Thoughts may be begotten totally or by parts

unusual_words.definition: Archaic spelling of original
unusual_words.context: Refers to the original source of perceptions: 'The originall of them all'

unusual_words.definition: Logical or sequential outcomes; train of ideas
unusual_words.context: Chapter title: 'OF THE CONSEQUENCES OR TRAIN OF IMAGINATIONS'

unusual_words.definition: Mental faculty for forming images or ideas
unusual_words.context: Discussed as derivable from sensation

unusual_words.definition: Use of language to communicate
unusual_words.context: Chapter about the origin and use of speech

unusual_words.definition: Faculty of logical thought and inference
unusual_words.context: Chapter title: 'OF REASON AND SCIENCE'

unusual_words.definition: Systematized knowledge
unusual_words.context: Paired with reason in chapter headings

unusual_words.definition: Done by will or choice
unusual_words.context: Appears in 'VOLUNTARY MOTIONS' chapter heading

unusual_words.definition: Movements or impulses; physical or mental
unusual_words.context: Discussed as internal beginnings of action (passions)

unusual_words.definition: Strong emotions or drives
unusual_words.context: Central to Hobbes' psychology and political theory

unusual_words.definition: Made clear; manifested
unusual_words.context: Used regarding how passions are shown by speeches

unusual_words.definition: Goals or purposes
unusual_words.context: Chapter on 'ENDS OR RESOLUTIONS OF DISCOURSE'

unusual_words.definition: Determinations or conclusions
unusual_words.context: Treated as outcomes of discourse

unusual_words.definition: Archaic spelling of virtues
unusual_words.context: Chapter title: 'OF THE VERTUES, COMMONLY CALLED INTELLECTUALL'

unusual_words.definition: Archaic spelling of intellectual
unusual_words.context: Used in the heading on intellectual virtues

unusual_words.definition: Failings or shortcomings
unusual_words.context: Opposites of virtues discussed in the text

unusual_words.definition: Those under sovereign authority
unusual_words.context: Chapter headings deal with liberty and duties of subjects

unusual_words.definition: Awareness or understanding gained by experience or study
unusual_words.context: One of the several subjects of knowledge Hobbes outlines

unusual_words.definition: Ability or capacity to act or command
unusual_words.context: Discussed as a key political and moral concept

unusual_words.definition: Value or merit
unusual_words.context: Appears in chapter heading on 'POWER, WORTH, DIGNITY, HONOUR'

unusual_words.definition: State of being worthy of respect
unusual_words.context: Grouped with worth and honour in content headings

unusual_words.definition: High respect or esteem; archaic spelling preserved elsewhere as HONOR’D
unusual_words.context: Used in dedication and chapter headings regarding reputation

unusual_words.definition: Archaic form of worthiness; deservingness
unusual_words.context: Used in dedication praising Sidney Godolphin's worthinesse

unusual_words.definition: Distinction or variation
unusual_words.context: Appears in heading 'OF THE DIFFERENCE OF MANNERS'

unusual_words.definition: Customs, habits, or modes of behavior
unusual_words.context: Discussed as part of human variability

unusual_words.definition: Belief and worship relating to divine
unusual_words.context: Treated as a subject and in relation to civil power

unusual_words.definition: Archaic spelling of natural
unusual_words.context: Used repeatedly in chapter headings and text

unusual_words.definition: State or circumstance
unusual_words.context: Used in heading 'OF THE NATURALL CONDITION OF MANKIND...'

unusual_words.definition: Human beings collectively
unusual_words.context: Central referent of Hobbes' political analysis

unusual_words.definition: Happiness or blessedness
unusual_words.context: Used in phrase 'their felicity and misery'

unusual_words.definition: Great suffering or unhappiness
unusual_words.context: Contrasted with felicity in natural condition chapter

unusual_words.definition: Formal agreement creating obligations
unusual_words.context: Foundational concept for the creation of the commonwealth

unusual_words.definition: Individual human agents or legal persons
unusual_words.context: Used in chapter heading 'OF PERSONS, AUTHORS, AND THINGS PERSONATED'

unusual_words.definition: Creators or originators (literal or legal)
unusual_words.context: Appears in the same chapter heading concerning personation

unusual_words.definition: Archaic form of personated or personified
unusual_words.context: Used in chapter heading about persons and what they represent

unusual_words.definition: Origins or reasons for events
unusual_words.context: Chapter headings include 'OF THE CAUSES, GENERATION, AND DEFINITION'

unusual_words.definition: Process of origin or creation
unusual_words.context: Appears in the contents describing generation of commonwealth

unusual_words.definition: A statement of the meaning of a term
unusual_words.context: Used where Hobbes sets down conceptual definitions

unusual_words.definition: Order of inheriting authority
unusual_words.context: Treated in chapters on kinds of commonwealth and succession

unusual_words.definition: Territorial rule or sovereignty
unusual_words.context: Discussed in chapters on dominion paternal and despotic

unusual_words.definition: Archaic spelling of paternal; relating to a father
unusual_words.context: Used in heading 'OF DOMINION PATERNALL, AND DESPOTICALL'

unusual_words.definition: Archaic spelling of despotic; tyrannical rule
unusual_words.context: Paired with paternal dominion in contents

unusual_words.definition: Freedom from arbitrary restraint
unusual_words.context: Addressed in chapter 'OF THE LIBERTY OF SUBJECTS'

unusual_words.definition: Archaic spelling of systems
unusual_words.context: Used in heading 'OF SYSTEMES SUBJECT, POLITICALL, AND PRIVATE'

unusual_words.definition: Archaic spelling of political
unusual_words.context: Used in chapter headings regarding political systems

unusual_words.definition: Archaic spelling of public
unusual_words.context: Used in relation to public ministers in state apparatus
